medications.drug_class: topical imidazole antifungal with a mild (low-potency) topical corticosteroid
medications.indication_rationale: A short course of a topical antifungal with an added mild corticosteroid treats the fungus in the fissured web space while rapidly settling the acute inflammation and pain — the source note's prescribed treatment for the fissure. In telehealth this is obtainable via a pharmacist or the reviewing GP; apply only after the web space has been gently cleansed and dried, and only a low-potency steroid combination (a potent steroid alone would worsen a fungal infection).
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer to the painful fissure/web space twice daily (dose per source note; do not exceed)
medications.duration: 3 days to the fissure (per source note)
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not use a potent topical corticosteroid alone on this fungal eruption
Stop and seek review if signs of secondary bacterial infection develop (ascending spreading redness, red streaks, thick pus, fever)

medications.indication_rationale: Fungicidal topical antifungal applied to the entire sole and all web spaces to clear the whole dermatophyte reservoir (including the moccasin plantar involvement), not just the fissure — the source note's prescribed maintenance antifungal.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to the entire sole and all web spaces once daily (dose per source note)

medications.drug_name: Topical antifungal foot/shoe powder (e.g. tolnaftate or miconazole powder) for footwear decontamination
medications.indication_rationale: Dusting the work boots with an antifungal powder kills residual spores in the footwear and reduces reinfection of a heavily-occluded, sweaty foot — per the source note's patient-education advice.
medications.dose_route_frequency: dust inside the work boots regularly per product directions

medications.indication_rationale: An unopposed potent topical steroid worsens and masks a dermatophyte infection (tinea incognito) and thins already-macerated fissured skin — a dangerous error of commission. Only a low-potency steroid combined with an antifungal is appropriate for the acutely inflamed fissure.

No fever, no ascending spreading erythema, no red streaks and no frank purulence — there is no secondary bacterial cellulitis to treat. Prescribing antibiotics for the serous exudate and malodour of a macerated fissure is over-treatment; reserve strictly for documented secondary cellulitis.

A localised interdigital tinea pedis responds to topical therapy; the source note used topical treatment only. Systemic terbinafine is reserved for extensive, refractory or nail-involving moccasin disease that fails topical treatment — not first-line here, and requires in-person assessment and baseline checks before prescribing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep the web spaces scrupulously dry: after every shower gently pat (not rub) the toes fully dry with a tissue or use a cool hair-dryer on the web spaces, and do not forcefully pull the toes apart.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Dermatophytes need a warm moist occluded surface; eliminating interdigital moisture is as important as the cream, lets the fissure close and prevents recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Switch to 100% cotton or moisture-wicking socks and change them halfway through each work shift; air out and rotate footwear and avoid the sealed work boots where possible while the fissure heals.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reducing occlusion and sweat removes the environment the fungus needs and offloads the painful fissure.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Treat the work boots with an antifungal powder to kill residual spores, and continue the plantar/web-space antifungal for the full 2-4 weeks even after the fissure feels better.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The footwear and the moccasin plantar reservoir are sources of reinfection; treating them and completing the antifungal course prevents relapse.

patient_education_points.point: This is a long-standing fungal skin infection (athlete's foot) that weakened the skin between your toes; the heavy sweating in your work boots waterlogged the skin until it split. It is common and treatable, not an emergency.

The two most important things are keeping the skin between your toes bone-dry and using the antifungal cream — a short course of the combined antifungal/anti-inflammatory cream on the painful crack, and the antifungal cream over the whole sole and web spaces for the full 2-4 weeks.

The sharp pain and weeping from the crack should improve a lot within 48-72 hours, but the thick flaky skin on the sole needs many weeks of continuous antifungal cream to fully clear. Apply the cream thinly and do not force the toes apart.

Because the skin is broken and open, you should be seen in person soon (within a day or two, and by about a week at the latest) so a clinician can look at the crack and check no infection is taking hold.

Get urgent help if the top of your foot becomes bright red, hot and swollen, if red streaks travel up your foot or leg, or if you develop a fever or uncontrollable shivering — these suggest a bacterial infection entering through the crack.

follow_up_plan.interval: prompt in-person GP review within 24-72 hours to inspect the fissure and check for early infection, and routine review by about 7 days to confirm the fissure has closed
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: closure of the interdigital fissure, response of the tinea to topical treatment, any early secondary bacterial infection, and the state of the plantar/web-space scaling
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: ascending hot spreading redness or red streaks up the foot/leg, thick or foul purulent discharge, fever or rigors, or severe worsening pain — seek same-day/emergency care

must_include_items: Recognise a common interdigital tinea pedis with a mechanical fissure (not an emergency) from the classic history/video pattern
Instruct patient-performable measures: keep the web spaces bone-dry (gentle patting, cool-air drying), off-load the foot, and reduce occlusive-footwear/sweat exposure

Advise a topical antifungal to the whole sole/web spaces (with, at most, a short low-potency antifungal/steroid combination for the inflamed fissure) rather than a potent steroid alone or oral antibiotics
Actively screen for, and safety-net against, secondary bacterial cellulitis / sepsis through the open fissure with explicit T4/T5 triggers
Arrange PROMPT in-person GP review (within ~24-72 hours, by ~7 days at the latest) given the open broken-skin fissure
acceptable_alternatives: A topical antifungal alone (e.g. terbinafine/miconazole) with the anti-inflammatory deferred, plus drying, is acceptable
Deferring the exact prescription to the reviewing GP/pharmacist rather than specifying a combination product sight-unseen is acceptable
Same-day (T3) in-person review is an acceptable, slightly more cautious disposition given the open fissure and is not scored as an error
Routine (T1) GP review by ~7 days with drying + a topical antifungal and an explicit cellulitis safety-net is acceptable for this systemically-well immunocompetent patient
errors_of_omission: Failing to advise keeping the web spaces dry / the drying measures
Failing to screen for or safety-net secondary bacterial cellulitis and systemic sepsis through the open fissure
Failing to arrange any in-person review of an open, weeping, broken-skin fissure (pure self-care only)
Treating only the fissure and not the whole plantar/web-space dermatophyte reservoir for the required weeks
errors_of_commission: Reflexively escalating a systemically-well patient with a negative cellulitis screen straight to 000/ED (over-triage)
Recommending a potent topical corticosteroid alone on a fungal eruption
Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics with no signs of secondary infection
Prescribing a systemic antifungal first-line for a localised cutaneous tinea pedis
Dismissing the presentation as trivial and giving no treatment, follow-up or safety-net for the open fissure
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) recognising the interdigital tinea pedis and advising drying + a topical antifungal, (b) an explicit secondary-infection safety-net with T4/T5 triggers for the open fissure, and (c) arranging in-person review of the broken skin. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED, or a potent-steroid-only / empiric-antibiotic answer, is a scored error regardless of other content; so is giving no treatment, follow-up or safety-net at all.
